title: Canon Aplio Series Ultrasound System - System Fails to Boot or Power-On Failure
description: Addresses system failure to power on caused by external power, battery issues, connections, or software initialization faults before assuming internal hardware failure.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety
steps.instructions: Confirm no patient is actively being imaged. Move any patient to a functional ultrasound system if imaging is urgent.

steps.title: Check Power Source
steps.instructions: Verify the power outlet is live by testing with a known working device. Ensure any extension cords or power strips are functional and rated for the system. Expected Outcome: System receives power; indicator lights may respond.

steps.title: Inspect Power Cable and Connections
steps.instructions: Check that the system power cable is fully seated at both ends. Look for visible damage, fraying, or bent pins. Expected Outcome: Firm, secure connection with no visible damage.

steps.title: Examine System Battery or UPS (if applicable)
steps.instructions: If using a backup battery or UPS, ensure it is charged and functioning. Remove the battery and attempt AC-only startup if supported. Expected Outcome: System powers on with AC power or indicates battery fault.

steps.title: Check External Indicators
steps.instructions: Observe any LEDs on the control panel or power supply for error codes. Expected Outcome: Normal power-on light sequence or specific fault code.

steps.title: Attempt Soft Reset / Power Cycle
steps.instructions: Disconnect power for 2–3 minutes. Reconnect and attempt startup. Expected Outcome: System initializes normally.

steps.title: Verify Environmental Factors
steps.instructions: Ensure ambient temperature and humidity are within operational limits. Remove any external devices that may interfere with boot (USB drives, external storage). Expected Outcome: System powers on without external interference.

CCR.Complaint: System will not power on when plugged into the room outlet.
CCR.Cause: External power and connections verified; soft reset did not resolve issue.
CCR.Resolution: Device removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent for repair.

helpfulDetails: Power cable condition and connections checked
Final device status: Out of Service

title: Canon Aplio Series Ultrasound System - Probe Not Recognized or Port Damage
description: Addresses probe detection failures caused by connection issues, port damage, or software recognition faults before assuming internal system failure.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety
steps.instructions: Remove the probe from any patient. If imaging is urgent, transition the patient to another system.

steps.title: Inspect Probe Connection
steps.instructions: Examine the probe connector for bent or broken pins, debris, or moisture. Expected outcome: Clean, intact connector with no visible damage.

steps.title: Verify Port Integrity
steps.instructions: Inspect the ultrasound system port for bent pins, debris, or corrosion. Expected outcome: Port appears intact and clean.

steps.title: Swap Probe / Port
steps.instructions: If possible, connect a known-good probe to the port, or connect the suspect probe to a different system. Expected outcome: Determines whether the issue is probe-specific or port-specific.

steps.title: Check Cable and Locking Mechanism
steps.instructions: Ensure probe cable is fully seated and locking latch is engaged. Expected outcome: Probe securely connected; system may now recognize the probe.

steps.title: Power Cycle System
steps.instructions: Turn off the ultrasound, wait 30–60 seconds, and power it back on. Expected outcome: System initializes probe recognition sequence.

steps.title: Software Recognition / Self-Test
steps.instructions: Run the system’s built-in probe self-test or diagnostics if available. Expected outcome: Probe passes or fails test; logs may indicate connection errors.

steps.title: Observe Indicator Lights / Messages
steps.instructions: Look for system warnings, error codes, or status lights on port or probe. Expected outcome: Confirms whether the system detects the probe or port fault.

CCR.Complaint: Probe not recognized on Canon Aplio system; error message displayed.
CCR.Cause: Inspection revealed bent pins in the system port and possible probe connector wear.
CCR.Resolution: System removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and sent to biomedical repair for port evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Which probe and port were tested
Indicator lights or error messages observed
Any unusual sounds, heat, or odor
Final device status (e.g., removed from service, pending repair)

title: Canon Aplio Series Ultrasound System - Image Quality Degradation or Artifacts
description: Addresses poor image quality, artifacts, or signal degradation caused by probe issues, settings, connections, or environmental factors before assuming internal system failure.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety
steps.instructions:
Remove the probe from the patient.
If imaging is urgent, move the patient to another functional system.

steps.title: Check Probe Connection
steps.instructions:
Ensure the probe is securely connected to the correct port.
Inspect the probe connector for bent pins, debris, or visible damage.
Correct connection issues and retest imaging.

steps.title: Swap or Test Probe
steps.instructions:
If possible, connect a known working probe to the same port.
If image quality improves, the original probe may be faulty.

steps.title: Verify System Settings
steps.instructions:
Check gain, depth, focus, and preset settings.
Reset to default imaging presets if unusual settings are detected.
Poor settings can mimic artifacts.

steps.title: Inspect Cables and Accessories
steps.instructions:
Examine probe cables for kinks, breaks, or excessive wear.
Ensure all extension cables or adapters are properly seated.

steps.title: Check Environmental Factors
steps.instructions:
Remove sources of electrical interference near the system.
Verify proper grounding and outlet integrity.

steps.title: System Software Check
steps.instructions:
Restart the system and reattempt imaging.
Ensure the software version is current; outdated software may cause image artifacts.

steps.title: Observe for Recurring Issues
steps.instructions:
Document any patterns: specific probes, imaging modes, or patient types.
Note unusual sounds, excessive heat, or warning indicators.

CCR.Complaint: Clinical staff reported streaking artifacts and poor image quality on abdominal scans.
CCR.Cause: Probe connector showed slight pin misalignment and settings were incorrect.
CCR.Resolution: Re-seated the probe, corrected settings to default, and retested; image quality restored.

helpfulDetails: No unusual heat, smell, or sounds

title: Canon Aplio Series Ultrasound System - DICOM / PACS Transfer Failure
description: Addresses failed study transfers to PACS caused by network, configuration, or software issues before assuming internal system or hardware failure.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety
steps.instructions:
Confirm no patient requires immediate imaging.
Move any patient needing urgent imaging to another system.

steps.title: Verify Network Connectivity
steps.instructions:
Check that the ultrasound system is properly connected to the hospital network.
Look for link/activity lights on network ports or Wi-Fi indicators.
Expected outcome: Active network connection indicated; if not, resolve network issue first.

steps.title: Test PACS Server Accessibility
steps.instructions:
Ping or traceroute the PACS server from the system’s network interface.
Confirm PACS server IP and hostname are reachable.
Expected outcome: Successful response; failures indicate network routing or firewall issue.

steps.title: Confirm DICOM Configuration
steps.instructions:
Check AE Title, PACS IP, and port settings in the Aplio system.
Ensure they match the PACS configuration.
Expected outcome: Correct configuration; incorrect settings must be updated.

steps.title: Examine Recent Study Logs
steps.instructions:
Access the system’s DICOM log for failed transfer attempts.
Note any error codes or messages (e.g., AE Title mismatch, timeout).
Expected outcome: Identify whether failure is system-side or PACS-side.

steps.title: Retry Transfer
steps.instructions:
Select a test image or study and manually retry sending to PACS.
Expected outcome: Successful transfer confirms issue was temporary; failure requires escalation.

steps.title: Swap or Test Network Cables
steps.instructions:
If on wired connection, try an alternate CAT6 cable and port.
Expected outcome: Resolves connectivity issues caused by damaged cables.

steps.title: Restart Ultrasound System
steps.instructions:
Reboot the system to refresh network services.
Expected outcome: Transfer may succeed if the issue was software-related.

CCR.Complaint: Studies fail to transfer to PACS; users report “Transfer Failed” errors.
CCR.Cause: Network configuration mismatch identified; PACS unreachable from system.
CCR.Resolution: Corrected AE Title, IP, and port settings; verified successful transfer to PACS.

helpfulDetails: Network port activity and link lights
Final status of test study transfer

title: Canon Aplio Series Ultrasound System - Touchscreen or Control Panel Errors
description: Addresses unresponsive or erratic touchscreen and control panel behavior caused by calibration drift, connection issues, software faults, or environmental interference.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Confirm the system is not actively imaging a patient. If urgent imaging is needed, move the patient to another available system.

steps.title: Check for External Interference
steps.instructions: Ensure no strong magnetic fields or nearby devices could affect touchscreen performance. Remove any cleaning wipes or protective films that may obstruct touch response.

steps.title: Inspect Connections
steps.instructions: Verify the control panel cable and connectors are fully seated. Check for visible damage to the ribbon or harness connecting the panel to the main unit.

steps.title: Perform Software Restart
steps.instructions: Reboot the ultrasound system using proper shutdown procedure. Observe if touchscreen and control panel respond correctly after restart.

steps.title: Calibrate Touchscreen
steps.instructions: Access system settings to initiate touchscreen calibration. Follow on-screen prompts and test response accuracy.

steps.title: Update Software / Firmware
steps.instructions: Check for available system updates addressing touchscreen or control panel issues. Apply updates following manufacturer instructions.

CCR.Complaint: Touchscreen intermittently fails to respond during exams; control panel buttons occasionally do not register input.
CCR.Cause: Touchscreen calibration was off, and control panel ribbon was slightly loose.
CCR.Resolution: Performed touchscreen calibration and reseated control panel connectors; system tested normal afterwards.

helpfulDetails: Control panel cable and connector status
Software version and firmware updates applied

title: Canon Aquilion Series CT Scanner - Gantry Rotation or Vibration Faults
description: Gantry rotation errors, vibration, abnormal noise, or scan interruption caused by external obstruction, table contact, setup, or mechanical faults.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Stop the exam if gantry vibration, abnormal noise, rotation fault, or scan interruption occurs during patient scanning. Action: Remove the patient from the scanner when safe; Move the patient to another CT scanner if the exam is clinically urgent; Do not attempt repeated scans if vibration or abnormal gantry noise is present. Expected outcome: Patient is safely removed from potential mechanical risk.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Fault
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact symptom before taking corrective action. Action: Ask staff what occurred; Check whether the issue happened during gantry rotation, helical scan, scout scan, or system startup; Record any displayed error message or fault code; Note whether the gantry stopped, vibrated, clicked, scraped, or made a grinding sound. Expected outcome: The failure pattern is clearly identified.

steps.title: Check for Patient, Table, or Accessory Contact
steps.instructions: Physical contact can cause gantry rotation faults or vibration alarms. Action: Inspect the gantry opening; Confirm the table, mattress, straps, IV lines, arm supports, positioning aids, or patient accessories are not contacting the gantry; Check that nothing is hanging into the scan field or rotation path. Expected outcome: No obstruction or contact is present. If contact or obstruction is found, remove it, reposition the table or accessory, and retest only when safe. If the issue clears, stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: Inspect the Area Around the Gantry
steps.instructions: External objects near the gantry can create noise, vibration, or safety concerns. Action: Check for loose objects near the gantry base; Look for dropped supplies, covers, cables, cleaning items, or debris; Verify no panels are visibly loose or displaced. Expected outcome: The gantry area is clear and free of loose items.

steps.title: Check Gantry Covers and External Panels
steps.instructions: Loose covers may vibrate during rotation and sound like an internal failure. Action: Visually inspect gantry covers and trim panels; Look for cracked, shifted, or loose panels; Do not remove covers unless permitted by site policy and service authorization. Expected outcome: Covers are secure with no visible damage. If a cover is loose or damaged, remove the scanner from service and escalate for repair.

steps.title: Check Table Position and Alignment
steps.instructions: Improper table positioning can cause scan aborts, contact, or alignment-related rotation faults. Action: Move the table fully out of the gantry; Check for smooth table travel; Confirm the tabletop is centered and not visibly skewed; Verify the table moves without rubbing, binding, or abnormal noise. Expected outcome: Table movement is smooth and does not interfere with the gantry.

steps.title: Review Recent Room or Equipment Changes
steps.instructions: New equipment or recent service work can introduce clearance or vibration issues. Action: Ask whether the room was recently cleaned, serviced, moved, or modified; Check for new accessories, injector lines, ceiling-mounted equipment, floor mats, or positioning devices; Confirm nothing was placed too close to the gantry or table path. Expected outcome: No recent external change explains the issue.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled System Restart
steps.instructions: A software or motion-control fault may clear after a proper restart. Action: Ensure no patient is on the table; Follow site-approved shutdown and startup procedure; Allow the system to fully initialize; Observe whether the fault returns during startup or standby. Expected outcome: System restarts normally with no immediate gantry fault. If the fault clears and no abnormal noise or vibration is present, continue with caution and document the event.

steps.title: Attempt Limited Non-Patient Functional Check Only If Safe
steps.instructions: Do not test aggressively if grinding, scraping, burning smell, visible damage, or severe vibration occurred. Action: With no patient present, perform only approved basic system checks; Observe for abnormal vibration, noise, rotation hesitation, or fault recurrence; Stop immediately if the gantry sounds abnormal or the fault returns. Expected outcome: Gantry rotation is smooth, quiet, and free of faults. If abnormal vibration or noise returns, stop testing and remove the scanner from service.

steps.title: Check Environmental and Facility Conditions
steps.instructions: Facility issues can contribute to vibration or unstable scanner operation. Action: Verify room temperature is within normal operating range; Check for water leaks, unusual heat, or HVAC issues near the scanner; Confirm no facility work is causing floor vibration nearby; Check for electrical events reported by staff. Expected outcome: Environment appears stable and suitable for CT operation.

steps.title: Determine Whether the Scanner Is Safe to Return to Use
steps.instructions: Only return the CT scanner to service if no fault returns and no abnormal mechanical symptoms are present. Action: Confirm no active gantry fault; Confirm no abnormal noise, vibration, smell, or visible damage; Confirm staff can operate the system normally; Document all checks performed. Expected outcome: Scanner is either safely returned to service or removed from service for repair.

CCR.Complaint: CT staff reported the Canon Aquilion scanner stopped during scan setup with gantry vibration and abnormal rotation noise.
CCR.Cause: External inspection found no patient accessory contact, table obstruction, loose room equipment, or visible gantry cover issue; fault returned during non-patient system check.
CCR.Resolution: Scanner was removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and escalated to Canon service for gantry rotation system evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Exact error message or fault code
Whether the fault occurred during scout, helical scan, startup, or rotation test
Whether table or accessories contacted the gantry
Any unusual smell, heat, scraping, or grinding
Whether the issue cleared after restart

title: Canon Aquilion Series CT Scanner - Tube or Generator Errors
description: Tube, generator, exposure, or high-voltage errors that prevent scan initiation or interrupt CT operation.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Stop the scan attempt and confirm the patient is safe before troubleshooting. Action: Do not continue repeated exposure attempts. Remove the patient from the scanner if the system cannot safely complete the exam. Notify CT staff that the unit should not be used until the issue is evaluated. Expected Outcome: Patient exposure risk is controlled and the CT scanner is not repeatedly forced into failed exposure attempts.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Error
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact error message, code, or system behavior. Action: Ask CT staff when the error occurred: startup, warm-up, scout, axial scan, helical scan, or during exposure. Record the displayed error message or code. Note whether the system aborted exposure, inhibited exposure, shut down, or displayed a generator/tube warning. Expected Outcome: The reported tube or generator issue is confirmed and documented accurately.

steps.title: Check for Active Patient or Exam Status Issues
steps.instructions: Make sure the system is not locked in a failed or incomplete exam state. Action: Confirm whether an exam is still open. Have CT staff properly end or close the failed exam if safe to do so. Return the system to a normal ready state if possible. Expected Outcome: If the issue was caused by an incomplete exam state or software lockout, the system may return to normal operation. If this resolves the issue, document the finding and stop.

steps.title: Check Emergency Stop and Room Safety Interlocks
steps.instructions: Exposure may be inhibited if a safety circuit is active. Action: Verify that the CT gantry emergency stop is not pressed. Check operator console emergency stop status. Confirm scan room doors are fully closed if connected to interlock monitoring. Look for any system message indicating exposure inhibit, door open, or safety interlock active. Expected Outcome: All safety interlocks are normal and exposure is not being blocked externally. If this resolves the issue, document the reset or interlock correction and stop.

steps.title: Confirm Stable Facility Power
steps.instructions: Tube and generator faults can occur if incoming power is unstable or interrupted. Action: Verify the CT system is connected to normal facility power. Ask if there were recent power events, generator transfers, brownouts, breaker trips, or UPS alarms. Check for power-related alarms at the CT power distribution area, if accessible and within Clinical Engineering responsibility. Do not open energized CT power cabinets unless qualified and authorized. Expected Outcome: No obvious external power issue is present. If a facility power issue is found, involve Facilities or the appropriate electrical support team before further scanner troubleshooting.

steps.title: Perform a Controlled System Restart if Appropriate
steps.instructions: Temporary software or communication faults may clear after a proper restart. Action: Coordinate with CT staff. Ensure no patient is in the scanner. Follow the site-approved shutdown and startup process for the Canon Aquilion system. Allow the system to fully initialize before retesting. Expected Outcome: The scanner boots normally and tube/generator errors clear if caused by a temporary communication or software state issue. If this resolves the issue, document the restart and successful return to service.

steps.title: Check Tube Warm-Up Status
steps.instructions: Tube or generator errors may occur if warm-up is incomplete, skipped, or interrupted. Action: Ask CT staff whether the tube warm-up was completed. Confirm whether the error occurred during warm-up or immediately after. Have qualified CT staff perform the normal tube warm-up procedure if the system allows it and no hard fault is active. Expected Outcome: Warm-up completes without fault and the system returns to ready status. If warm-up fails again, stop repeated attempts and escalate.

steps.title: Check for Tube Heat or Cooling-Related Warnings
steps.instructions: The system may inhibit exposure if tube heat limits or cooling conditions are outside safe range. Action: Check the console for tube heat, cooling, or waiting messages. Ask whether the scanner was used heavily before the fault. Allow the system to cool if a heat-related message is present. Verify room temperature and airflow are within expected clinical operating conditions. Check for blocked cabinet vents or abnormal room heat. Expected Outcome: If the fault was heat-related, the system may recover after cooling and returning to normal operating temperature. If the same error returns after cooldown, remove the scanner from service and escalate.

steps.title: Inspect External Gantry and Cabinet Conditions
steps.instructions: Look for obvious signs of overheating, electrical odor, or abnormal operation. Action: Visually inspect around the gantry, generator cabinet, and system electronics cabinets. Check for unusual sounds, burnt smell, excessive heat, visible fluid intrusion, or tripped external indicators. Do not remove covers or access high-voltage areas during basic troubleshooting. Expected Outcome: No obvious external hazard is present. If unusual heat, smell, smoke, or electrical noise is present, stop immediately, remove the scanner from service, and escalate urgently.

steps.title: Check Operator Console Communication
steps.instructions: Some generator or tube messages may be triggered by communication loss between the console, gantry, and generator subsystem. Action: Confirm the operator console is responsive. Check whether gantry controls, table controls, and system status screens respond normally. Note whether the error appeared after a network, software, or console freeze. Restart only if safe and site procedure allows. Expected Outcome: The console and scanner subsystems communicate normally. If communication errors continue with tube/generator faults, escalate for service evaluation.

steps.title: Attempt Only One Controlled Functional Retest
steps.instructions: After external checks and a proper restart, perform a limited retest only if the system appears safe. Action: Ensure no patient is in the scanner. Have authorized CT staff perform the appropriate system readiness check or phantom/non-patient test if allowed by site policy. Observe whether the same tube/generator error returns. Expected Outcome: System completes readiness testing without exposure faults. If the error returns, stop troubleshooting and remove the scanner from service.

CCR.Complaint: CT staff reported that the Canon Aquilion scanner displayed a tube/generator error during scan initiation and would not complete exposure.
CCR.Cause: Verified recurring exposure inhibit after checking emergency stops, room interlocks, system status, cooling condition, and facility power with no external cause identified.
CCR.Resolution: Removed CT scanner from service, labeled unit Out of Service, documented error behavior, and escalated to Canon service for tube/generator system evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Exact error message or code displayed
When the error occurred: startup, warm-up, scout, axial, helical, or exposure
Whether the exam was interrupted or exposure was inhibited
Emergency stop and door interlock status
Facility power condition or recent power events
Any unusual sounds, odor, heat, or visible cabinet indicators
Whether the error returned after retest

title: Canon Aquilion Series CT Scanner - Detector Calibration or Image Artifacts
description: Image artifacts, calibration failures, ring artifacts, streaking, shading, or inconsistent CT image quality caused by setup, calibration, or detector-related issues.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot image quality issues during an active patient scan unless the scan has been safely stopped by clinical staff. Remove the scanner from clinical use if image quality may affect diagnosis. Notify CT staff that the system should not be used until the artifact is verified and corrected. Use another CT scanner if available. Expected outcome: No patient is scanned on a system producing questionable or unreliable images.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Artifact or Calibration Issue
steps.instructions: Confirm exactly what the user is seeing before assuming detector failure. Ask CT staff what type of artifact appeared. Check whether the issue occurred on one patient, multiple patients, phantom images, or all scans. Identify if the artifact appears as rings, streaks, bands, shading, noise, missing data, or reconstruction distortion. Review whether the issue started after warm-up, calibration, software restart, power event, tube change, service visit, or room temperature change. Expected outcome: The problem is clearly defined and repeatable, or isolated to a specific scan condition. Why it matters: Some artifacts are caused by patient positioning, metal, contrast, protocol settings, or reconstruction choices rather than detector hardware.

steps.title: Remove Patient-Related and External Causes First
steps.instructions: Artifacts may be caused by items in the scan field. Confirm the patient table, straps, immobilizers, IV poles, oxygen tubing, ECG leads, jewelry, clothing snaps, or external devices were not in the scan field. Ask whether the artifact appeared in the same location on multiple patients. If safe and appropriate, have CT staff repeat a test scan using a manufacturer-approved phantom or known-good setup. Expected outcome: Artifacts caused by external objects or patient setup are identified and corrected. If this resolves the issue, document the finding and return the scanner to service after image quality is confirmed.

steps.title: Check Scan Protocol and Reconstruction Settings
steps.instructions: Incorrect protocol or reconstruction choices can mimic system image problems. Have CT staff verify the selected protocol, reconstruction kernel, slice thickness, field of view, dose settings, iterative reconstruction settings, and metal artifact reduction options. Compare the problematic scan to a known-good protocol. Check whether the issue occurs only with one protocol or all protocols. Expected outcome: If the issue is protocol-specific, clinical applications or CT leadership should review the protocol before equipment repair is pursued. Why it matters: Clinical Engineering should rule out setup and configuration issues before escalating to detector or reconstruction hardware concerns.

steps.title: Check System Readiness and Error Messages
steps.instructions: Detector calibration issues may be accompanied by system warnings or failed readiness checks. Review the scanner console for active system messages, calibration prompts, detector errors, air calibration failures, reconstruction errors, or image quality warnings. Record the exact message text, error code, time, and sequence of events. Check whether the scanner allows normal scan preparation. Expected outcome: Any system-generated fault is captured accurately for service documentation.

steps.title: Confirm Gantry and Bore Are Clear
steps.instructions: Objects inside the gantry opening can affect image quality or interfere with calibration scans. Inspect the gantry opening and table area for loose items, pads, positioning aids, contrast tubing, cables, cleaning materials, or covers. Confirm no object is blocking the scan field or contacting moving components. Verify the table top is clean and properly positioned. Expected outcome: The scan field is clear and free of external causes.

steps.title: Verify Environmental Conditions
steps.instructions: CT detector calibration can be affected by temperature instability or room environmental changes. Check whether the CT room temperature is within normal operating expectations. Ask staff if HVAC problems, room overheating, humidity issues, recent power loss, or room door propping occurred. Check for unusual heat, odors, fan noise, or blocked ventilation around system cabinets. Expected outcome: Environmental causes are ruled out or corrected before deeper troubleshooting. Why it matters: Detector systems and image calibration depend on stable environmental conditions.

steps.title: Perform a Proper System Restart if Appropriate
steps.instructions: Temporary software or reconstruction issues may clear after a controlled restart. Coordinate with CT staff to ensure the scanner is not in use. Follow the site-approved shutdown and startup process. Allow the system to fully boot and complete readiness checks. Do not interrupt startup, calibration, or self-test routines. Expected outcome: If the artifact was caused by a temporary software or communication issue, the scanner returns to normal operation. If this resolves the issue, perform image verification before returning to service.

steps.title: Run User-Accessible Calibration or Air Calibration Only if Allowed
steps.instructions: Calibration should only be performed if it is part of normal operator-accessible workflow or approved Clinical Engineering procedure. Have qualified CT staff or authorized service personnel perform the appropriate detector calibration, air calibration, or image quality check using the approved process. Do not bypass service protections or perform unauthorized service-level calibrations. Record whether calibration passes, fails, or aborts. Expected outcome: Calibration completes successfully and image quality returns to normal, or the failure is confirmed for escalation. Why it matters: Failed calibration after external causes are ruled out may indicate detector, DAS, reconstruction, temperature, or calibration data issues.

steps.title: Perform Phantom or Image Quality Verification
steps.instructions: Before returning the scanner to service, image quality should be verified. Have CT staff perform a known-good phantom or site-approved image quality test. Review whether artifacts persist in the same location or pattern. Compare to previous acceptable image quality if available. Confirm no new error messages appear after calibration. Expected outcome: Images are clinically acceptable and artifacts are not reproducible. If artifacts remain, remove the scanner from service.

steps.title: Identify Artifact Pattern for Escalation
steps.instructions: The artifact pattern can help determine the likely area of concern. Document whether the artifact is ring-shaped, straight streaking, banding, shading, excessive noise, missing data, intermittent, protocol-specific, present on all scans, present only after warm-up, or present only after calibration failure. Expected outcome: The service vendor or imaging specialist receives useful detail instead of a vague “bad image” complaint.

steps.title: Check for Recent Service, Updates, or Events
steps.instructions: Recent changes often explain image quality problems. Ask whether there was recent PM, tube service, detector calibration, software update, power outage, HVAC work, gantry service, or protocol change. Review recent work orders for related CT issues. Check whether the artifact began immediately after one of these events. Expected outcome: A possible trigger is identified and included in the repair escalation.

CCR.Complaint: CT staff reported ring artifacts and failed detector calibration on the Canon Aquilion Series CT scanner during morning startup.
CCR.Cause: External scan field, patient setup, room conditions, protocol selection, and system restart were checked; artifact persisted during phantom verification.
CCR.Resolution: Scanner was removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and escalated to Canon service for detector calibration and image quality evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Console error message or calibration failure text
Whether artifact appeared on patient images, phantom images, or both
Whether issue affects all protocols or one protocol
Recent power outage or service activity
Unusual heat, smell, fan noise, or cabinet alarms

title: Canon Aquilion Series CT Scanner - Table Movement or Alignment Issues
description: Table motion, height, indexing, or alignment issues caused by obstruction, setup, power, interlock, or positioning faults.

steps.title: 1. Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: Do not troubleshoot table movement issues with a patient on the table unless movement is required for immediate patient safety. Action: Stop the scan or setup process. Move the patient to another CT system or safe patient surface if table movement is unreliable. Confirm the table is not unexpectedly moving, lowering, or locked in an unsafe position. Expected outcome: Patient is safe and the CT table is not being operated under unsafe conditions.

steps.title: 2. Verify the Reported Table Issue
steps.instructions: Confirm exactly what the table is doing before deeper troubleshooting. Action: Ask whether the issue involves vertical movement, horizontal movement, indexing accuracy, table alignment, docking, or table motion during scan setup. Observe whether the problem occurs from the gantry controls, operator console, table-side controls, or all controls. Note any displayed error messages, warnings, or interlock indicators. Expected outcome: The failure mode is clearly identified instead of treated as a general table problem.

steps.title: 3. Check for Physical Obstruction Around the Table
steps.instructions: External obstruction is one of the first things to rule out. Action: Inspect around the table base, cradle, floor track area, side covers, foot end, and gantry opening. Remove linens, positioning aids, straps, cables, IV tubing, stools, trash cans, or other items that may restrict table travel. Confirm nothing is caught between the moving tabletop and fixed table structure. Expected outcome: Table moves normally if the issue was caused by obstruction. If resolved, return the system to service after confirming normal movement and stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: 4. Check Emergency Stop and Safety Interlocks
steps.instructions: A pressed emergency stop or active safety interlock can prevent table movement. Action: Inspect all accessible emergency stop buttons at the gantry, console, and room locations. Reset any activated emergency stop according to site procedure. Check for open covers, panel interlocks, collision detection messages, or safety-related warnings. Expected outcome: Table movement returns if a safety stop or interlock was active. If resolved, verify normal table motion before clinical use and stop troubleshooting.

steps.title: 5. Confirm CT System Power and Ready State
steps.instructions: The table may not move if the scanner is not fully initialized or ready. Action: Verify the CT system, gantry, operator console, and table are powered on. Confirm the system is not stuck in startup, shutdown, service mode, scan abort, or error recovery. Review console messages for table-related or gantry communication warnings. Expected outcome: Table controls respond once the CT system reaches a normal ready state.

steps.title: 6. Test Table Movement From Multiple Control Points
steps.instructions: This helps separate a local control issue from a larger table drive or system issue. Action: Try table movement from the gantry/table controls. Try movement from the operator console if available. Compare vertical, horizontal, and indexing functions separately. Do not force movement or repeatedly command the table if it stalls, binds, or makes abnormal noise. Expected outcome: If one control point fails but another works, suspect a local control, keypad, switch, or communication issue. If all control points fail, suspect a table drive, power, interlock, or system-level issue.

steps.title: 7. Inspect Table Accessories and Patient Positioning Devices
steps.instructions: Incorrectly installed accessories can interfere with table travel or alignment. Action: Check head holders, arm boards, body cradles, immobilization devices, extension boards, and positioning pads. Confirm accessories are fully seated and compatible with the table setup. Remove nonessential accessories and retest table movement if safe. Expected outcome: Table movement or alignment returns to normal if an accessory was interfering with travel or positioning.

steps.title: 8. Check Table Load and Patient Positioning Conditions
steps.instructions: Excessive load, uneven weight distribution, or positioning near travel limits can affect movement. Action: Confirm the table is not overloaded beyond site-approved operating limits. Make sure weight is centered and the tabletop is not being side-loaded. If safe, return the table toward a neutral position and retest movement without unnecessary load. Expected outcome: Table movement improves if the issue was related to loading, imbalance, or travel limit conditions.

steps.title: 9. Verify Table Position, Home, and Indexing Behavior
steps.instructions: Alignment issues may be caused by the table not returning to a known position. Action: Check whether the system recognizes table position correctly. Attempt normal table home, zero, or reset functions available to the operator workflow. Compare indicated table position with the actual physical position. Watch for inconsistent indexing, incorrect displayed position, or failure to stop at expected locations. Expected outcome: If the table position corrects after normal reset/home operation, verify accuracy before returning to use. If displayed position does not match actual movement, remove the scanner from service.

steps.title: 10. Check for Gantry-to-Table Alignment Problems
steps.instructions: Poor alignment can affect patient centering, scan setup, and image accuracy. Action: Inspect table alignment relative to the gantry opening and laser positioning references. Confirm the tabletop is not visibly skewed, shifted, loose, or misaligned. Verify the table docks or aligns normally with the scan plane. Do not perform patient scanning if table position accuracy is questionable. Expected outcome: Table alignment appears stable and consistent. If alignment is visibly off or inconsistent, escalate for service evaluation.

steps.title: 11. Listen for Abnormal Mechanical Sounds
steps.instructions: Noise can indicate mechanical binding, drive strain, worn components, or obstruction. Action: Operate the table only briefly and safely. Listen for grinding, clicking, slipping, squealing, banging, or motor strain. Stop testing immediately if movement is jerky, uneven, or worsening. Expected outcome: Smooth, quiet movement suggests the issue may have been external or intermittent. Abnormal sound with movement suggests mechanical or drive-related failure requiring repair.

steps.title: 12. Perform a Controlled System Restart Only If Safe
steps.instructions: A software or communication fault may temporarily prevent table control. Action: Confirm no patient is on the table. Follow site procedure to restart the CT system. After restart, verify table movement, position readback, alignment, and console status. Do not restart during an active clinical emergency unless directed by site protocol. Expected outcome: Table movement returns if the issue was a temporary software or communication fault. If resolved, perform functional checks before clinical use and stop troubleshooting.

CCR.Complaint: CT staff reported the Canon Aquilion table would not move horizontally during patient setup and the table position did not appear to match the console display.
CCR.Cause: Clinical Engineering found no external obstruction, emergency stop activation, accessory interference, or power issue, but table indexing remained inconsistent after restart.
CCR.Resolution: Scanner was removed from service, labeled Out of Service, and escalated to CT service/vendor support for table motion and position feedback evaluation.

helpfulDetails: Whether the issue involved vertical, horizontal, or indexing movement
Whether the table failed from gantry controls, console controls, or both
Any table, gantry, or interlock messages displayed
Whether the table position display matched actual table position
Whether restart or home/reset restored function

title: Canon Aquilion Series CT Scanner - Cooling System or Temperature Alarms
description: Cooling or temperature alarms caused by blocked airflow, room conditions, coolant issues, chiller problems, or heat-related system faults.

steps.title: Ensure Patient Safety First
steps.instructions: If the scanner is in use or a patient is on the table, stop scanning safely and follow department protocol. Move the patient off the scanner if continued troubleshooting could delay care or create risk. Expected outcome: Patient care is not dependent on a scanner that may be overheating or unable to complete imaging safely.

steps.title: Verify the Reported Alarm
steps.instructions: Confirm the exact cooling or temperature alarm shown on the Canon Aquilion console. Check whether the alarm appears: At startup; During tube warm-up; During scanning; After heavy patient volume; Continuously, even when idle. Expected outcome: The alarm behavior is confirmed and documented. Why it matters: Intermittent heat alarms after heavy scanning may point to cooling capacity or room temperature issues, while immediate alarms may indicate sensor, chiller, coolant, or internal hardware faults.

steps.title: Stop Scanning and Allow the System to Stabilize
steps.instructions: If the scanner reports overheating or temperature out of range, do not continue scanning. Allow the system to cool according to site policy and scanner status indicators. Expected outcome: Temperature-related warnings may clear after the system returns to an acceptable operating range. If this resolves the issue: Return the scanner to service only after verifying normal startup, no active temperature alarms, and acceptable operation.

steps.title: Check Room Temperature and HVAC Conditions
steps.instructions: Verify that the CT room temperature is within the site’s required operating range. Look for: Room unusually warm; HVAC failure; Closed or blocked vents; Recent facilities temperature complaints; Excess heat from nearby equipment. Expected outcome: CT room environmental conditions are normal. Why it matters: CT scanners generate significant heat and depend on stable room cooling. A room HVAC issue can trigger scanner cooling alarms even when the scanner itself is not defective.

steps.title: Inspect External Airflow Around the Gantry and Cabinets
steps.instructions: Check for blocked intake or exhaust areas around the gantry, operator console, power cabinets, and equipment cabinets. Look for: Linens, boxes, carts, or supplies blocking vents; Dust buildup on external vent areas; Equipment pushed too close to walls; Panels not fully seated. Expected outcome: Airflow paths are clear and nothing external is restricting ventilation. If this resolves the issue: Clear obstructions, allow the system to stabilize, and verify the alarm does not return.

steps.title: Check for Obvious Cooling Unit or Chiller Issues
steps.instructions: If the site uses an external chiller or dedicated cooling unit, inspect it from the outside only. Check for: Power present to the chiller; Chiller running normally; Displayed chiller alarms; Unusual noise; Visible leaks; Blocked ventilation around the chiller; Temperature or flow warnings. Expected outcome: The cooling unit is powered, running, and not showing obvious external faults. Why it matters: A scanner temperature alarm may originate from the cooling system, not the CT gantry itself.

steps.title: Check for Visible Coolant Leaks
steps.instructions: Inspect around the gantry, cooling cabinet, chiller, and visible coolant lines where accessible. Do not open internal covers or disconnect coolant lines unless trained and authorized. Look for: Puddles; Wet flooring; Drips; Crusty residue near fittings; Low coolant warning on the cooling unit. Expected outcome: No visible coolant leak is present. If a leak is found: Remove the scanner from service and escalate for repair.

steps.title: Check Power to Supporting Cooling Equipment
steps.instructions: Verify that cooling-related equipment is connected to the correct power source and that breakers or disconnects have not been unintentionally switched off. Check only accessible facility power sources and approved disconnects. Expected outcome: Cooling support equipment has stable power. Why it matters: Loss of power to the chiller or cooling cabinet can create temperature alarms even if the CT scanner console still powers on.

steps.title: Review Recent Work or Environmental Changes
steps.instructions: Ask whether there was recent activity that may have affected cooling. Examples: HVAC maintenance; Electrical work; Chiller service; Room rearrangement; Cabinet moved or blocked; High scan volume; Recent water leak or facility issue. Expected outcome: Any recent change is identified and corrected if related.

steps.title: Restart Only if Safe and Appropriate
steps.instructions: If there is no leak, no burning smell, no overheating condition, and no active cooling equipment fault, perform a controlled system restart according to site procedure. Expected outcome: Temporary software or sensor communication faults may clear. If the alarm clears: Monitor the system during startup and warm-up before returning it to clinical use. If the alarm returns: Do not continue repeated restarts. Escalate.

steps.title: Check for Unusual Heat, Smell, or Noise
steps.instructions: While the system is idle, observe for abnormal signs. Look for: Excessive heat near cabinets; Burning smell; Loud fan noise; No fan noise where fan operation is normally expected; Chiller vibration; Repeated coolant pump cycling; Alarm returning shortly after reset. Expected outcome: No signs of active overheating or equipment distress are present. If heat, odor, or abnormal noise is present: Remove from service immediately.

steps.title: Confirm Final Scanner Status
steps.instructions: Before returning the scanner to service, confirm: No active cooling or temperature alarms; Room temperature is acceptable; Chiller or cooling unit is operating normally; No visible leaks; No abnormal odor, heat, or noise; Scanner completes startup and readiness checks. Expected outcome: Scanner is safe and ready for clinical use.

CCR.Complaint: CT staff reported a Canon Aquilion scanner cooling system alarm during patient scanning, preventing further exams.
CCR.Cause: Room temperature was elevated and airflow around the equipment cabinet was partially blocked by stored supplies.
CCR.Resolution: Removed airflow obstruction, notified facilities regarding room temperature, allowed system to stabilize, verified no active temperature alarms, and returned scanner to service.

helpfulDetails: Whether alarm occurred at startup, idle, warm-up, or during scanning
Room temperature at time of failure
Any unusual heat, odor, or noise
